label: Formula 10.5a
nearby_text: nces is $ a_n\theta_i $ (Equation 9.21a). Because $ S_i $ is a measure of the amount of polymorphism, we denote it by $ P_i $ to conform to the standard notation for MK tests. Thus, under neutrality, $$ \frac{D_{a}}{D_{s}}=\frac{2t\mu_{a}}{2t\mu_{s}}=\frac{2t\mu f n_{a}}{2t\mu n_{s}}=f\frac{n_{a}}{n_{s}},\quad\frac{P_{a}}{P_{s}}=\frac{S_{a}}{S_{s}}=\frac{a_{n}\theta_{a}}{a_{n}\theta_{s}}=\frac{4N_{e}\mu f n_{a}}{4N_{e}\mu n_{s}}=f\frac{n_{a}}{n_{s}} $$ (10.5a) where the subscript a denotes replacement (amino-acid changing) sites, and s denotes silent sites. Hence, under the equilibrium neutral model, we expect that, on average, $$ D_{a}/D_{s}=P_{a}/P_{s} $

subsection: Estimating the Fraction, $ \alpha $, of Substitutions That are Adaptive
nearby_text: To reduce bias from correlations between f and $ N_{e} $, Smith and Eyre-Walker (2002) suggested the estimator $$ \widehat{\overline{\alpha}}_{SEW}=1-\frac{\overline{D_{s}}}{\overline{D_{a}}}\overline{\left(\frac{P_{a}}{P_{s}+1}\right)} $$ (10.9a) where the second term is the average of the quantity $ P_a/(P_s + 1) $ over the sampled genes. label: Formula 10.11c
subsection: Estimating the Rate, $ \lambda $, of Adaptive Substitutions
nearby_text: en by Equation 4.3b. Substituting the observed values of $ D_{s} $ and $ P_{s} $ for their expected values and rearranging provides a simple method-of-moments estimator for the scaled divergence time $$ \widehat{\tau}=\left(a_{m}+a_{n}\right)\frac{D_{s}}{P_{s}}-\left(\frac{1}{m}+\frac{1}{n}\right) $$ (10.11c)

Substituting the observed values of $ D_{s} $ and $ P_{s} $ for their expected values and rearranging provides a simple method-of-moments estimator for the scaled divergence time $$ \\widehat{\\tau}=\\left(a_{m}+a_{n}\\right)\\frac{D_{s}}{P_{s}}-\\left(\\frac{1}{m}+\\frac{1}{n}\\right) $$ (10.11c)"
}

label: Formula 10.11d
subsection: Estimating the Rate, $ \lambda $, of Adaptive Substitutions
nearby_text: Using this estimate yields $ \widehat{t} = 2N_e\widehat{\tau} $, and substituting into Equation 10.11a yields $$ \widehat{\lambda}=\frac{\alpha d_{a}}{2N_{e}\widehat{\tau}} $$ (10.11d)

subsection: Estimating the Rate, $ \lambda $, of Adaptive Substitutions

label: Formula 10.14a
section: THE SAWYER-HARTL POISSON RANDOM FIELD MODEL
nearby_text: Recall (Equation 7.13b) Wright’s (1938b) classic result for the amount of time that a new segregating mutation (with selection coefficient s) spends in the interval $ (x, x + dx) $, $$ \phi(x\mid N_{e}s)=\frac{1-e^{-2\gamma(1-x)}}{1-e^{-2\gamma}}\frac{1}{x(1-x)}dx $$ (10.14a) where $x$ is the frequency of the derived allele. In the limit as $\gamma \to 0$, this reduces to $dx/x$, recovering Watterson's expression for the (unfolded) site-frequency spectrum (SFS) for neutra

  "nearby_text": "Recall (Equation 7.13b) Wright’s (1938b) classic result for the amount of time that a new segregating mutation (with selection coefficient s) spends in the interval $ (x, x + dx) $, $$ \\phi(x\\mid N_{e}s)=\\frac{1-e^{-2\\gamma(1-x)}}{1-e^{-2\\gamma}}\\frac{1}{x(1-x)}dx $$ (10.14a) where $x$ is the frequency of the derived allele. In the limit as $\\gamma \\to 0$, this reduces to $dx/x$, recovering Watterson's expression for the (unfolded) site-frequency spectrum (SFS) for neutra"
}

label: Formula 10.14b
section: THE SAWYER-HARTL POISSON RANDOM FIELD MODEL
nearby_text: .25a) (Bierne and Eyre-Walker 2004; Eyre-Walker et al. 2006; Welch 2006; Boyko et al. 2008; Eyre-Walker and Keightley 2009; Keightley and Eyre-Walker 2012). Under this model, the expected SFS becomes $$ \phi(x\mid N_{e},\boldsymbol{\Delta})=\int\phi(x\mid N_{e}s)\varphi(s\mid\boldsymbol{\Delta})ds $$ (10.14b)

label: Formula 10.16c
section: THE SAWYER-HARTL POISSON RANDOM FIELD MODEL

section: Using Molecular Data to Detect Selection: Signatures from Multiple Historical Events: Introduction
nearby_text: The fraction of adaptive substitutions is the rate of adaptive substitutions divided by the total rate of substitutions (adaptive plus neutral), $$ \alpha=\frac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu p_{0}} $$ (10.16b)

label: Formula 10.16d
section: Using Molecular Data to Detect Selection: Signatures from Multiple Historical Events: Introduction
nearby_text: final result emerges from Equation 10.16a. Because $ \mu p_b $ is the rate of beneficial mutation, which (in keeping with our notation from Chapter 8) we denote by $ \mu_b $, Equation 10.16a becomes $$ \lambda=2\gamma\mu_{b} $$ (10.16d) which immediately suggests the Bachtrog estimator (2008), $$ \mu_{b}=\frac{\lambda}{2\gamma} $$ (10.16e)

nearby_text: n mutate to one of nine other codons following a single base change (Figure 10.4).   "nearby_text": "n mutate to one of nine other codons following a single base change (Figure 10.4). The model given by Goldman and Yang (1994) defines the following relative rates for movement between codons i and j, $$ Q_{ij}=\\left\\{\\begin{aligned}&0&if i and j differ at more than one position\\\\&\\pi_{j}&for a silent transversion\\\\&\\kappa\\pi_{j}&for a silent transition\\\\&\\omega\\pi_{j}&for a replacement transversion\\\\&\\omega\\kappa\\pi_{j}&for a replacement transition\\end{aligned}\\right.\\quad for1\\leq i,j\\leq61 $$ (10.18)"
}

label: Formula 10.19
nearby_text: the likelihood-ratio approach (LW Appendix 4). The key to these likelihood calculations is that $ \mathbf{P}(t) $, the codon state matrix at time t, is related to the instantaneous rate matrix, Q, by $$ \mathbf{P}(t)=\exp(\mathbf{Q}t) $$ (10.19)

label: Formula 10.22b
nearby_text: being neutral (with probability $ p_0 $), deleterious (with probability $ p_d $), or advantageous (with probability $ p_b = 1 - p_n - p_d $). Within each class there is a fixed selective value, with $$ \omega^{(k)}=\left\{\begin{aligned}&0&\quad\text{deleterious class}\\ &1&\quad\text{neutral class}\\ &\omega>1&\quad\text{positively selected class}\end{aligned}\right. $$ (10.22b)

label: Formula 10.24a
subsection: Bayesian Estimators of Sites Under Positive Selection
nearby_text: Suppose there are k classes, with each class having a different associated $ \omega $. The posterior probability that a specific codon is in fitness class i is $$ \Pr(class i\mid D)=\frac{\Pr(D\mid class i)\Pr(class i)}{\Pr(D)}=\frac{\Pr(D\mid\omega_{i})\Pr(\omega_{i})}{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\Pr(D\mid\omega_{i})\Pr(\omega_{i})} $$ (10.24a) where $D$ is the pattern of codons for that site in the tree, and the prior $\operatorname{Pr}(\text{class } i)$—the values for $p_0$, $p_b$, and $p_d$—is estimated by maximum likelihood.   "nearby_text": "Suppose there are k classes, with each class having a different associated $ \\omega $. The posterior probability that a specific codon is in fitness class i is $$ \\Pr(class i\\mid D)=\\frac{\\Pr(D\\mid class i)\\Pr(class i)}{\\Pr(D)}=\\frac{\\Pr(D\\mid\\omega_{i})\\Pr(\\omega_{i})}{\\sum_{i=1}^{k}\\Pr(D\\mid\\omega_{i})\\Pr(\\omega_{i})} $$ (10.24a) where $D$ is the pattern of codons for that site in the tree, and the prior $\\operatorname{Pr}(\\text{class } i)$—the values for $p_0$, $p_b$, and $p_d$—is estimated by maximum likelihood. The case of"

Hence $$ \omega=\frac{K_{a}}{K_{s}}=\frac{f\mu_{s}+2\gamma p_{b}\mu_{s}}{\mu_{s}}=f+2\gamma p_{b}=p_{0}+2\gamma p_{b} $$ (10.25a) so that very strong, or frequent, selection $ (\gamma p_b > 1) $ is required for $ \omega > 1 $.   "nearby_text": "ctively neutral substitutions accrue at a rate of $ f\\mu_s $, while (Equation 8.24a) beneficial substitutions accrue at rate $ \\lambda = (2N\\mu_b)(2sN_e/N) = 2(2N_e)s\\mu_b = 2\\gamma p_b\\mu_s $. Hence $$ \\omega=\\frac{K_{a}}{K_{s}}=\\frac{f\\mu_{s}+2\\gamma p_{b}\\mu_{s}}{\\mu_{s}}=f+2\\gamma p_{b}=p_{0}+2\\gamma p_{b} $$ (10.25a) so that very strong, or frequent, selection $ (\\gamma p_b > 1) $ is required for $ \\omega > 1 $. Similarly, we can rearrange this equation to solve for $ \\gamma $, $$ \\gamma=\\frac{\\omega-f}{2p_{b}}=\\"

        "latex": "\\omega=\\frac{K_{a}}{K_{s}}=\\frac{f\\mu_{s}+2\\gamma p_{b}\\mu_{s}}{\\mu_{s}}=f+2\\gamma p_{b}=p_{0}+2\\gamma p_{b}",
        "label": "Formula 10.25a",
        "section": "Using Molecular Data to Detect Selection: Signatures from Multiple Historical Events: Introduction",

  "nearby_text": "{b}=p_{0}+2\\gamma p_{b} $$ (10.25a) so that very strong, or frequent, selection $ (\\gamma p_b > 1) $ is required for $ \\omega > 1 $. Similarly, we can rearrange this equation to solve for $ \\gamma $, $$ \\gamma=\\frac{\\omega-f}{2p_{b}}=\\frac{\\omega-p_{0}}{2p_{b}} $$ (10.25b)"
}

label: Formula 10.25c
section: Using Molecular Data to Detect Selection: Signatures from Multiple Historical Events: Introduction
subsection: CONNECTING THE PARAMETERS OF ADAPTIVE EVOLUTION
nearby_text: \gamma = 400$ only gives $\omega = 1.3$, which is a sufficiently small deviation to avoid detection in many cases. Finally, to connect $\alpha$ and $\omega$, from Equations 10.16b and 10.25a, we have $$ \alpha=\frac{2\gamma p_{b}}{2\gamma p_{b}+p_{0}}=\frac{2\gamma p_{b}}{\omega} $$ (10.25c) which can alternately be expressed as $$ \alpha\omega=2\gamma p_{b},\qquad\mathrm{a n d}\qquad\omega=\frac{2\gamma p_{b}}{\alpha} $$ (10.25d)

  "nearby_text": "\\gamma = 400$ only gives $\\omega = 1.3$, which is a sufficiently small deviation to avoid detection in many cases.
